enrichment.short_description: Codeberg's CI is the continuous integration service built into Codeberg, the German nonprofit's Forgejo-based Git hosting platform. It runs on Woodpecker CI, an open-source, container-based pipeline engine, and lets projects hosted on Codeberg automate testing, builds, and deployments. Access is free but gated by a manual approval process to protect shared infrastructure. Codeberg also offers Forgejo Actions, a GitHub-Actions-style alternative, in open alpha.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free, community-funded (no paid tiers; shared compute is rationed via manual approval and fair-use limits)
enrichment.company_size: Volunteer-run nonprofit association (Codeberg e.V.), 1,300+ association members as of late 2025, small paid and volunteer operations team
enrichment.seo_title: Codeberg CI Review 2026: Woodpecker CI for Codeberg
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What Codeberg's CI is, how the free Woodpecker CI-based pipeline works, how to get access, its limits, and how it fits into Codeberg.org.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Codeberg's CI is the continuous integration and continuous delivery service that Codeberg, the nonprofit German Git-hosting platform, provides to projects hosted on codeberg.org. Codeberg itself is operated by Codeberg e.V., a registered nonprofit association founded in September 2018 and headquartered in Berlin, Germany; the public Codeberg.org platform launched in January 2019 as a community-owned, donation- and membership-funded alternative to GitHub and GitLab, built on the open-source Forgejo software, a Gitea fork. Codeberg's CI is powered by Woodpecker CI, an open-source, container-native pipeline engine that grew out of the original Drone CI project after Drone's license changed; Codeberg runs a shared, publicly reachable Woodpecker instance at ci.codeberg.org. Because compute resources are limited and funded entirely by community memberships and donations, Codeberg does not open CI access to every repository automatically. Instead, maintainers must fill out a request form describing their use case; a Codeberg volunteer reviews the request and manually approves suitable projects to prevent abuse of shared infrastructure. Once approved, a user logs into ci.codeberg.org with their existing Codeberg account and enables CI for individual repositories from the Woodpecker repo-list page. Pipelines are defined in YAML files stored in the repository, and steps run inside Docker containers, similar to other container-based CI systems. Codeberg's shared runners only support the linux/amd64 architecture; teams that need other architectures, faster builds, or more predictable throughput are encouraged to attach their own self-hosted Woodpecker agents, which Woodpecker supports natively. Because the service is run by volunteers on donated infrastructure, Codeberg is explicit that CI access is provided on an as-is, best-effort basis and can be paused, rate-limited, or interrupted for maintenance without notice; there is no SLA. Role-based access control on the shared instance is also more limited than a typical commercial CI product, which can be a friction point for larger teams collaborating on a single repository. In parallel with Woodpecker CI, Codeberg has been rolling out Forgejo Actions, a CI system built directly into the Forgejo software Codeberg runs, using a syntax intentionally close to GitHub Actions; as of 2026 this remains in a limited open-alpha rollout alongside Woodpecker rather than a full replacement for it. There is no separate product page, sales team, or paid tier for Codeberg's CI, since it is a feature of the Codeberg platform itself rather than a standalone commercial product; the operation is funded by the roughly 1,300-plus paying members of Codeberg e.V. plus one-off donations, and governance is community-driven, with members electing the association's presidium and board. For directory and comparison purposes, Codeberg's CI is best understood as a free, ethically-run, resource-constrained alternative to commercial hosted CI/CD services such as GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, or CircleCI, aimed at open-source maintainers and small teams who value data sovereignty, European hosting, and a nonprofit governance model over guaranteed throughput or enterprise support. Typical users are open-source library maintainers, small self-hosted software projects, and privacy- or Europe-conscious developers moving away from large US-based platforms. It is not designed for, and does not attempt to compete with, high-volume commercial CI workloads that need guaranteed concurrency or dedicated support contracts.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Codeberg's CI Works
paragraphs: Codeberg's CI runs on Woodpecker CI, an open-source, container-native pipeline engine hosted at ci.codeberg.org. Pipelines are defined in YAML files stored directly in the repository, and each step runs inside a Docker container, an approach familiar to anyone who has used Drone CI, GitLab CI, or similar container-based systems.

Because Codeberg's compute is donated and limited, only linux/amd64 builds run on the shared instance. Projects needing other architectures, more throughput, or predictable performance can attach their own self-hosted Woodpecker agents, which plug into the same Codeberg-hosted control plane.

long_form_blocks.heading: Access, Limits, and Forgejo Actions
paragraphs: Getting started requires filling out a request form; a Codeberg volunteer manually reviews and approves suitable use cases before granting access, a deliberate friction point meant to prevent abuse of shared resources funded by nonprofit memberships and donations.

Codeberg is upfront that the service has no SLA and can break or pause during maintenance. Role-based access control is also more limited than commercial CI products. In parallel, Codeberg has been rolling out Forgejo Actions, a GitHub-Actions-style CI system built into the Forgejo software itself, currently in limited open alpha alongside Woodpecker.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Codeberg's CI Is For
paragraphs: Codeberg's CI suits open-source maintainers, small self-hosted projects, and developers who prioritize European hosting and community governance over guaranteed uptime or enterprise support contracts.

It is not intended to compete with high-volume commercial CI platforms; teams with demanding concurrency or reliability needs are better served by paid providers or by attaching their own self-hosted Woodpecker agents to Codeberg's control plane.

faqs.question: Is Codeberg's CI free?
faqs.answer: Yes. It is funded by Codeberg e.V.'s nonprofit memberships and donations, with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: What CI engine does Codeberg use?
faqs.answer: Woodpecker CI, an open-source, container-based pipeline engine, hosted at ci.codeberg.org.

faqs.question: How do I get access to Codeberg's CI?
faqs.answer: Fill out a request form describing your use case; a Codeberg volunteer manually reviews and approves it.

faqs.question: Can I use my own build hardware with Codeberg's CI?
faqs.answer: Yes, Woodpecker supports self-hosted agents that teams can attach for specialized needs.

faqs.question: Does Codeberg's CI support architectures other than linux/amd64?
faqs.answer: Not on the shared runners; other architectures require a self-hosted agent.

faqs.question: What is Forgejo Actions and how does it relate to Woodpecker?
faqs.answer: Forgejo Actions is a GitHub-Actions-style CI system built into Forgejo itself, currently in limited open alpha alongside Woodpecker.

faqs.question: Who operates Codeberg's CI?
faqs.answer: Codeberg e.V., a German nonprofit association headquartered in Berlin, founded in 2018 with the platform launching in 2019.

faqs.question: Is there an SLA or guaranteed uptime for Codeberg's CI?
faqs.answer: No. Access is provided on an as-is, best-effort basis with no guaranteed uptime.

enrichment.short_description: Codecov (codecov.io) is a code coverage reporting and analytics platform that ingests test coverage reports from CI pipelines and turns them into pull-request comments, dashboards, and trend graphs. Founded in 2015, it was acquired by Sentry in 2022 and then by Harness (from Sentry) in 2026. It integrates with GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and most CI providers to show teams exactly which lines and files lack test coverage.
enrichment.pricing_model: Per-seat monthly or annual subscription with a free tier
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Developer plan); paid plans start at $5 per user per month (Team plan)
enrichment.company_size: Mid-size team operating as part of Harness; exact standalone headcount not publicly disclosed
enrichment.seo_title: Codecov Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What Codecov does, how its coverage reporting works, 2026 pricing plans, integrations, pros and cons, and who it is really built for.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Codecov, accessible at codecov.io and about.codecov.io, is a code coverage reporting and analytics platform used by software teams to understand how much of their codebase is exercised by automated tests. Rather than running tests itself, Codecov ingests coverage reports, in formats like lcov, Cobertura XML, or JaCoCo, generated by a team's existing test runner during a CI pipeline run, then processes and visualizes that data. Its signature feature is posting automated coverage comments and status checks directly on GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket pull requests, showing reviewers exactly which new or changed lines are untested before code is merged, alongside file-by-file and project-wide coverage percentages, diff coverage, and historical trend graphs. Codecov was founded in 2015 by Jerrod Engelberg and Eli Hooten and grew into one of the most widely used coverage tools in the open-source and enterprise CI/CD ecosystem, integrating with virtually every major CI provider, including GitHub Actions, CircleCI, Travis CI, Jenkins, GitLab CI, and Azure Pipelines, via a lightweight uploader CLI or GitHub Action. In November 2022, application-monitoring company Sentry acquired Codecov to combine coverage insight with error monitoring and broader code-quality tooling. In June 2026, Codecov was acquired again, this time by Harness, a software delivery platform, which positioned the deal as embedding coverage intelligence directly into CI/CD release pipelines and software delivery governance. Throughout these ownership changes, Codecov has kept its own branding, product, and self-serve pricing page live at about.codecov.io. As of 2026, Codecov offers four tiers. The Developer plan is free, capped at one user (unlimited for public open-source repositories) with up to 250 private-repo uploads per month, and includes PR comments, status checks, API access, patch coverage, bundle-size analysis, failed-test reporting, and Slack notifications. The Team plan costs 5 US dollars per user per month, caps out at 10 users, raises the private-repo upload allowance to 2,500 per month, and adds project-level coverage, coverage flags, components, carryforward flags, flaky-test detection, and Sentry integration. The Pro plan costs 12 US dollars per user per month, removes the user cap and the upload cap entirely, and includes everything in Team. Enterprise pricing is custom and adds a dedicated cloud deployment option, faster processing, Okta single sign-on, dedicated IP allowlisting, a customer success manager, a solutions architect, dedicated onboarding, and legal support for regulated environments. Codecov's core value proposition is reducing the risk of merging undertested code by making coverage visible and actionable inside the existing pull-request workflow rather than requiring engineers to check a separate dashboard. Features like flags and components let large monorepos break coverage down by service or team ownership, while carryforward flags prevent coverage numbers from artificially dropping when only part of a test suite runs on a given commit. Bundle analysis, a newer addition, extends the same trend-tracking approach to JavaScript bundle size rather than just test coverage. Codecov is commonly compared to Coveralls, Code Climate, SonarQube and SonarCloud, and Codacy; its main differentiators are its long track record, broad CI and language support, and deep GitHub and GitLab pull-request integration. Typical users range from individual open-source maintainers on the free tier to enterprise engineering organizations that use coverage thresholds as a merge gate in regulated or high-reliability codebases.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Codecov Works
paragraphs: Codecov does not run tests itself; it ingests coverage reports produced by a team's existing test runner during a CI job, then visualizes that data as pull-request comments, status checks, diff coverage, and historical trend graphs.

It supports common coverage formats such as lcov, Cobertura XML, and JaCoCo, and connects via a lightweight uploader CLI or GitHub Action to nearly every major CI provider and Git host, including GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, CircleCI, Jenkins, and Azure Pipelines.

long_form_blocks.heading: Codecov Pricing Explained
paragraphs: Codecov's pricing is per-seat: Developer is free with a 250-upload cap for private repos, Team is 5 US dollars per user per month for up to 10 users, and Pro is 12 US dollars per user per month with no user or upload cap.
Enterprise pricing is custom and adds a dedicated cloud option, Okta SSO, IP allowlisting, and dedicated onboarding and support for regulated or large organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Ownership: Sentry to Harness
paragraphs: Founded in 2015, Codecov was acquired by Sentry in November 2022 to pair coverage data with error monitoring, then acquired again by Harness in June 2026 to embed coverage intelligence into software delivery pipelines.
Despite two ownership changes, Codecov has retained its own product branding and self-serve pricing page, and continues to operate as a distinct product within its parent company.

features.title: Pull request coverage comments
features.description: Automated comments and status checks post directly on GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket pull requests.

features.title: Diff and patch coverage
features.description: Highlights exactly which new or changed lines in a pull request are untested.

features.title: Flags and components
features.description: Break coverage down by service, team, or test suite in large monorepos.

features.title: Carryforward flags
features.description: Prevents coverage numbers from falsely dropping when only part of a suite runs.

features.title: Flaky test detection
features.description: Identifies tests that intermittently pass and fail to reduce noisy CI signals.

features.title: Bundle analysis
features.description: Tracks JavaScript bundle size trends alongside test coverage.

features.title: Broad CI and Git host integrations
features.description: Works with GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, CircleCI, Jenkins, Azure Pipelines, and more.

features.title: API access
features.description: Programmatic access to coverage data for custom dashboards and reporting.

pros: Generous free tier for open-source and small private projects
Deep, native pull-request integration reduces workflow friction
Wide CI and language support via a simple uploader
Historical trend graphs make coverage regressions easy to spot
Enterprise options like SSO and dedicated cloud suit regulated teams
cons: Team plan is capped at 10 users, forcing a jump to Pro for growing teams
Per-seat pricing can get expensive for large engineering organizations
Requires an existing test suite and coverage-report generation; it does not write tests itself
Repeated ownership changes, from Sentry to Harness, create roadmap uncertainty
Advanced features like components and bundle analysis add a learning curve

faqs.question: Is Codecov free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Developer plan is free, with unlimited use for public open-source repositories.

faqs.question: What coverage report formats does Codecov support?
faqs.answer: Common formats including lcov, Cobertura XML, and JaCoCo, uploaded via CLI or GitHub Action.

faqs.question: Who owns Codecov now?
faqs.answer: Harness, which acquired Codecov from Sentry in June 2026.

faqs.question: Does Codecov run my tests for me?
faqs.answer: No, it ingests coverage reports generated by your existing test runner during CI.

faqs.question: What is the difference between the Team and Pro plans?
faqs.answer: Team is capped at 10 users and 2,500 monthly private uploads for 5 dollars per user per month; Pro removes both caps for 12 dollars per user per month.

faqs.question: Does Codecov support GitLab and Bitbucket, not just GitHub?
faqs.answer: Yes, Codecov integrates with GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket.

faqs.question: What are Codecov flags?
faqs.answer: A way to tag coverage data by test suite, service, or component for more granular reporting.

faqs.question: Can Codecov block a pull request from merging?
faqs.answer: Yes, via required status checks configured in the Git host based on Codecov's coverage results.

use_cases.title: Monorepo coverage governance
use_cases.description: Enterprises track coverage across many services using flags and components.

enrichment.short_description: CodedThemes is an Indian software vendor that designs and sells premium admin dashboard templates and UI kits for React, Angular, Vue, Bootstrap, Laravel, and CodeIgniter. Founded in 2017 and based in Surat, Gujarat, it operates a template marketplace with both free and paid products, with Able Pro, Berry, and Mantis as its best-known lines. Customers buy per-template licenses rather than a recurring platform subscription.
enrichment.pricing_model: One-time per-template licensing (Single Use, Extended Use, and Multi Extended Use license tiers), plus free templates
enrichment.starting_price: Free for free-tier templates; paid templates typically start in the tens of US dollars per license
enrichment.seo_title: CodedThemes Review 2026: Admin Templates and Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What CodedThemes sells, its Able Pro, Berry and Mantis template lines, license types, pricing structure, pros, cons and who should buy from it.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CodedThemes is a template and UI-kit vendor founded in 2017 and headquartered in Surat, Gujarat, India, that designs and sells admin dashboard templates for web application front ends. Rather than being a SaaS platform with recurring subscriptions, CodedThemes operates more like a specialized digital marketplace: it builds a library of dashboard UI products once, in multiple front-end frameworks, and sells licenses to individual developers, agencies, and companies who use them as the starting point for internal tools, SaaS admin panels, CRMs, analytics dashboards, and other back-office interfaces. Its templates are built and maintained for the most common modern stacks, including React, Angular, Vue.js, and Bootstrap or plain HTML, as well as backend-oriented variants for Laravel and CodeIgniter, so a team can pick the same visual design system regardless of which framework their application uses. The company's flagship product lines are Able Pro, available across React, Angular, Vue, and Bootstrap versions and positioned as its premium, most fully featured dashboard, Berry, a widely downloaded template with both free and pro tiers and also available for CodeIgniter, and Mantis, a newer, lighter Material-UI-inspired dashboard offered in free and paid variants. Alongside dashboards, CodedThemes also publishes landing pages, admin UI component kits, and Figma design-system files, and it maintains a blog covering front-end and admin-UI topics. Commercially, CodedThemes departs from typical SaaS pricing: rather than monthly seats, it sells templates under one of three license types. A Single Use License covers one product for one non-commercial or internal end product where the end customer is not charged for access. An Extended Use License covers a single project where the end user of that project can be charged, for example a client-facing paid product, which is the license required for most commercial SaaS builds. A Multi Extended Use License covers unlimited projects where end users can be charged, aimed at agencies or software vendors that want to reuse the same template across many client engagements without buying a new license each time. Prices are quoted in US dollars, inclusive of tax, and vary by template and license tier, with free versions of several templates, such as Berry Free and Mantis Free, available as a way to trial the code quality before buying the pro version. Because CodedThemes is a template vendor rather than an infrastructure or SaaS company, it does not have uptime SLAs, API rate limits, or usage-based billing in the way a hosting or backend platform would; its main obligations to customers are template updates, documentation, and license enforcement. Support is typically handled through a ticketing system or marketplace channels, since it distributes some products through its own store and others via marketplaces such as Envato and ThemeForest. Typical buyers are individual freelance developers building admin panels quickly, startups and agencies that need a professional-looking back-office UI without a dedicated design team, and product teams standardizing on Able Pro, Berry, or Mantis as their internal design-system starting point. Competitors in this space include other template marketplaces and specific dashboard-kit vendors selling similar Bootstrap, React, and Vue admin themes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Able Pro, Berry, and Mantis Template Lines
paragraphs: Able Pro is CodedThemes' flagship premium dashboard, available in React, Angular, Vue, and Bootstrap versions, giving teams a consistent design system regardless of framework choice.

Berry is a widely downloaded template line with free and pro tiers plus a CodeIgniter version, while Mantis is a newer, lighter Material-UI-inspired dashboard also offered in both free and paid variants.

long_form_blocks.heading: How CodedThemes Licensing Works
paragraphs: CodedThemes sells one-time licenses rather than subscriptions. A Single Use License covers a single non-commercial product; an Extended Use License covers one commercial project where end users can be charged.
A Multi Extended Use License covers unlimited projects and is designed for agencies and SaaS vendors reusing the same template across many client engagements without buying repeat licenses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Buys From CodedThemes
paragraphs: Typical buyers include freelance developers building admin panels quickly, startups and agencies needing a polished back-office UI without a dedicated design team, and product teams standardizing on Able Pro, Berry, or Mantis.
Free versions such as Berry Free and Mantis Free let developers evaluate code quality before committing to a paid license.

faqs.answer: CodedThemes' flagship premium dashboard template line, available in four frameworks.

faqs.answer: Yes, including Berry Free and Mantis Free.

faqs.answer: An Extended Use License for a single commercial project, or a Multi Extended Use License for multiple client projects.

enrichment.short_description: CodeFactor is an automated code review and code-quality tracking tool that connects to GitHub and Bitbucket to analyze every commit and pull request. It checks cyclomatic and cognitive complexity, code duplication, style violations, and language-specific anti-patterns, then summarizes results as letter grades from A through F for files, directories, and whole repositories. Founded in 2016 and based in Santa Monica, California, it targets teams wanting lightweight, zero-configuration quality feedback inside their existing Git workflow.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium, flat per-repository monthly or annual subscription for private repos, not per-user
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Community plan, unlimited public repos); paid Pro plans priced per private repository
enrichment.company_size: Very small, roughly 2 to 10 employees, self-funded with no reported outside investment
enrichment.seo_title: CodeFactor Review 2026: Pricing, Grades and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: How CodeFactor's automated code review and A-F grading system works, 2026 pricing plans, supported languages, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CodeFactor, at codefactor.io, is an automated code review and code-quality tracking service founded in 2016 by Darius Kriksciukaitis and Kestas Barzdaitis and headquartered in Santa Monica, California. It connects directly to a team's GitHub or Bitbucket account and, once authorized, begins analyzing code automatically on every new commit and pull request with no configuration required beyond connecting the repository. CodeFactor's core mechanic is turning static-analysis findings into a simple, easy-to-communicate letter grade, from A down to F, calculated from cyclomatic complexity, cognitive complexity, code duplication, style-guide violations, and known anti-patterns for each supported language. Grades are computed and shown at multiple levels of granularity, including individual files, directories, and the repository as a whole, and CodeFactor keeps a historical grade chart so a team can see at a glance whether quality is trending up or down over time, independent of any single pull request. The service supports a long list of languages and file types, including Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Vue, CSS, HTML, Shell, C#, Java, PHP, Dockerfile, Scala, Go, YAML, PowerShell, Ruby, Swift, C, C++, Kotlin, Dart, R, Haskell, and Groovy. On pull requests, CodeFactor posts inline status checks and PR decoration showing exactly which new issues were introduced, and it offers on-demand or automatic autofix for certain classes of reported issues, plus the ability to customize rule sets or ignore specific findings that a team judges not relevant to their codebase. CodeFactor integrates with GitHub and Bitbucket for source control, and with Slack and Microsoft Teams for notifications, making it easy to route new quality issues into a team's existing chat workflow. CodeFactor's pricing model is unusual among code-quality tools in that it is priced per repository rather than per user or per seat: the Community plan is entirely free and supports unlimited public repositories and unlimited users, with a quality badge, PR decoration, and full grade history at no cost, making it a common choice for open-source projects that want a visible quality signal without any budget. Paid tiers exist for private repositories: the Pro plan adds a limited number of private repos with a cap of two branches per repository and a maximum repository size of 2 gigabytes, while the Pro Max plan, marketed as the best-value option, removes the branch and repository-size caps and is subject to a fair-use policy instead. Both paid tiers include unlimited users and unlimited public repos at no extra cost, since CodeFactor's billing unit is the private repository, not the seat. Annual billing offers a discount of up to 20 percent versus paying monthly, and CodeFactor also offers a free Pro plan for verified students and for qualifying registered non-profits, plus a 20 percent discount on larger plans for non-profits that exceed the free thresholds. Compared to competitors like Codacy, Code Climate, DeepSource, or SonarCloud and SonarQube, CodeFactor's main differentiators are its zero-configuration setup, its instantly understandable A-F grading system, and its per-repository, rather than per-seat, pricing, which tends to make it cheaper for small teams with few repositories but many contributors, and comparatively more expensive for organizations with many small private repositories. Typical users are small to mid-size engineering teams, open-source maintainers, and individual developers who want a fast, low-friction quality signal on every pull request without adopting a heavier, more configurable static-analysis platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: How CodeFactor's A-F Grading Works
paragraphs: CodeFactor's analysis engine checks cyclomatic complexity, cognitive complexity, code duplication, style violations, and common anti-patterns for each supported language, then rolls those findings into a letter grade from A to F.

Grades are shown at the file, directory, and repository level, with a historical chart so teams can track whether quality is trending up or down over time rather than reacting only to individual pull requests.

long_form_blocks.heading: CodeFactor Pricing: Per-Repository, Not Per-Seat
paragraphs: Unlike most code-quality tools, CodeFactor bills by private repository rather than by user. The Community plan is free with unlimited public repos and unlimited users; Pro and Pro Max add private repos at increasing size and branch limits.
Annual billing saves up to 20 percent versus monthly, and students plus qualifying non-profits can get a free Pro plan, with larger non-profits receiving a 20 percent discount.

long_form_blocks.heading: Supported Languages and Integrations
paragraphs: CodeFactor supports more than 20 languages, including Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, C#, Go, Swift, Kotlin, and Dart, and connects to both GitHub and Bitbucket with zero-configuration setup.
Notifications route into Slack or Microsoft Teams, and CodeFactor can autofix certain reported issues on demand or automatically as part of the review workflow.

features.title: Automatic PR and commit analysis
features.description: Every new commit and pull request is analyzed automatically with no configuration needed.

features.title: A-F letter grade scoring
features.description: Quality is summarized as a letter grade for files, directories, and repositories.

features.title: Historical grade trend charts

faqs.question: What does CodeFactor's letter grade mean?
faqs.answer: It is a composite score based on complexity, code duplication, style violations, and anti-patterns.

faqs.question: Is CodeFactor free?
faqs.answer: Yes for unlimited public repositories and users; private repositories require a paid plan.

faqs.question: How is CodeFactor priced?
faqs.answer: Per private repository, not per user, unlike most code-quality competitors.

faqs.question: What languages does CodeFactor support?
faqs.answer: More than 20, including Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, Go, C#, Swift, and Kotlin.

faqs.question: Does CodeFactor integrate with Bitbucket as well as GitHub?
faqs.answer: Yes, both are supported.

faqs.question: Can CodeFactor automatically fix issues?
faqs.answer: Yes, autofix is available for certain issue types, either on demand or automatically.

faqs.question: Is CodeFactor free for students and nonprofits?
faqs.answer: Yes, a free Pro plan is offered for verified students and qualifying non-profits.

faqs.question: Who founded CodeFactor?
faqs.answer: Darius Kriksciukaitis and Kestas Barzdaitis founded it in 2016.

enrichment.short_description: codehooks.io is a backend-as-a-service platform that lets developers write serverless JavaScript, TypeScript, or Node.js backend logic and deploy it in seconds via a CLI, with a built-in NoSQL document database, key-value store, queues with background workers, cron jobs, and webhook handling included behind every endpoint. It is built by Codehooks AS, a Norwegian company founded by the team behind restdb.io, and launched publicly in 2024 with flat-rate pricing instead of per-request metering.
enrichment.pricing_model: Flat-rate tiered subscription, not metered per request, with a free development tier and paid add-ons
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Development plan); paid plans start at $19 per month (Pro plan)
enrichment.company_size: Small team; exact headcount not publicly disclosed, spun out of the restdb.io team
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What codehooks.io does, its serverless JavaScript backend and NoSQL database, 2026 pricing tiers, key features, pros, cons and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: codehooks.io is a backend-as-a-service platform that lets developers write and deploy complete API backends using plain JavaScript, TypeScript, or Node.js, without managing servers, containers, or build pipelines. It is built and operated by Codehooks AS, a company based in Norway founded by Knut Martin Tornes and colleagues who previously built restdb.io, an online database backend launched in 2016; codehooks.io itself launched publicly in 2024 as an evolution of restdb.io's low-code hooks engine, rebuilt from scratch into a more complete, code-first backend platform aimed at JavaScript and TypeScript developers. The platform's central idea is that a single deployed codehooks.io project already includes everything most backends need out of the box: a queryable NoSQL document database built on RocksDB with a MongoDB-like query language, a separate lightweight key-value store, background job queues with dedicated workers, cron-scheduled tasks that can trigger behind any endpoint, REST API routing, static file hosting, and first-class inbound and outbound webhook handling with signature verification and automatic retries, useful for receiving events from services like Stripe, Shopify, or GitHub and forwarding processed results to end users. Authentication is handled through JWT and JWKS integration plus encrypted API keys, so teams can protect endpoints without standing up a separate auth service. codehooks.io is explicitly CLI-first: developers write handler functions locally, then deploy with a terminal command, with deploys typically completing in under five seconds because there are no containers to build or infrastructure to provision per deploy. The platform also markets itself as agent-ready, meaning it is designed to be driven by AI coding agents such as Claude Code or Cursor, which can write handler code and run the deploy command directly, and a webhook inspector tool lets developers capture, inspect, and replay incoming webhook payloads during development and debugging. Commercially, codehooks.io differentiates itself from most serverless platforms by using flat-rate, predictable pricing instead of per-request or per-invocation metering: a team's bill does not change based on how many webhooks it processes or how often it deploys, within its plan's rate limits. The Development plan is free and includes 60 API requests per minute, 150 megabytes of database storage, and one developer seat, suitable for prototyping and small personal projects. The Pro plan costs 19 US dollars per month and raises the ceiling to 3,600 API requests per minute, 15 gigabytes of database storage, and three developer seats. The Team plan costs 99 US dollars per month, with 6,000 API requests per minute, 25 gigabytes of database storage, and six developer seats. Beyond these tiers, teams can add capacity a la carte: additional developer seats cost 5 US dollars each, extra database storage is billed at 0.10 US dollars per gigabyte, file storage at 0.02 US dollars per gigabyte, custom domains at 10 US dollars, and dedicated spaces at 10 US dollars. Typical users are indie developers and small startups building an MVP API backend quickly, teams that need to reliably receive and process third-party webhooks from payment providers, e-commerce platforms, or CI systems, and developers who want a JavaScript-native alternative to heavier backend-as-a-service platforms like Firebase or Supabase for use cases centered on background jobs, cron automation, and webhook orchestration rather than a full relational database and auth suite.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Ships Behind Every codehooks.io Endpoint
paragraphs: Every deployed codehooks.io project includes a queryable NoSQL document database built on RocksDB with a MongoDB-like query language, a lightweight key-value store, background job queues with workers, and cron-scheduled tasks.

It also handles inbound and outbound webhooks with signature verification and automatic retries, useful for receiving events from Stripe, Shopify, or GitHub, plus JWT and JWKS authentication and encrypted API keys for protecting endpoints.

long_form_blocks.heading: Flat-Rate Pricing Instead of Per-Request Billing
paragraphs: Unlike most serverless platforms, codehooks.io charges flat monthly rates rather than metering per request or invocation, so a team's bill stays predictable within its plan's rate limits.
Plans scale by API requests per minute, database storage, and developer seats, with a la carte add-ons available for extra developers, storage, custom domains, and dedicated spaces.

long_form_blocks.heading: CLI-First and AI-Agent-Ready Deployment
paragraphs: Developers write handler functions locally and deploy via a terminal command, with deploys typically completing in under five seconds since there are no containers to build.
The platform is designed to be driven by AI coding agents such as Claude Code or Cursor, and includes a webhook inspector for capturing and replaying payloads during development.

Many backend primitives, including database, queue, cron, and webhooks, are bundled into one platform

Built by an experienced team with a decade of backend-SaaS history via restdb.io
Designed to work well with AI coding agents for automated backend generation
cons: Young product, launched in 2024, with a smaller track record than established BaaS platforms
Rate limits on requests per minute are relatively low on entry-level plans
Smaller ecosystem and community than Firebase, Supabase, or AWS Lambda
No relational or SQL database option; NoSQL only
Team size and long-term company scale are not publicly disclosed

faqs.question: What is codehooks.io?
faqs.answer: A serverless JavaScript and TypeScript backend platform with a built-in NoSQL database, queues, cron, and webhooks.

faqs.question: Who built codehooks.io?
faqs.answer: Codehooks AS, based in Norway, built by the team behind restdb.io.

faqs.question: Is codehooks.io billed per request?
faqs.answer: No, it uses flat-rate tiered pricing rather than per-request metering.

faqs.question: What database does codehooks.io use?
faqs.answer: A built-in NoSQL document database on RocksDB with a MongoDB-like query language.

faqs.question: Does codehooks.io support cron jobs?
faqs.answer: Yes, cron-scheduled tasks can trigger behind any endpoint.

faqs.question: Can codehooks.io receive and process webhooks?
faqs.answer: Yes, with signature verification and automatic retries for inbound and outbound webhooks.

faqs.question: Is there a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Development plan is free with limited request-rate and storage quotas.

faqs.question: Does codehooks.io work with AI coding agents?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is designed to be agent-ready for tools like Claude Code and Cursor.

enrichment.short_description: CodeKeep is a code snippet manager that lets developers save, organize, and share reusable pieces of code and text, with folder- and label-based organization, browser and editor extensions, and a built-in screenshot generator for sharing snippets visually. It targets individual developers and small teams who want a lightweight, cloud-based alternative to scattered notes files or gists. Paid plans start at 4.99 US dollars per month.
enrichment.starting_price: Paid plans start at $4.99 per month; a free tier is also available
enrichment.seo_title: CodeKeep Review 2026: Pricing and Snippet Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What CodeKeep does, how its code snippet organization works, pricing starting at 4.99 dollars per month, key features, pros, cons and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CodeKeep, at codekeep.io, is a code snippet management tool built for developers who want a dedicated place to save, organize, and reuse pieces of code and text rather than scattering them across notes apps, chat messages, or personal gists. The product's own positioning describes it as combining the quick-capture simplicity of a notes app like Google Keep with the code-sharing sensibility of GitHub, applied specifically to small, reusable fragments rather than full repositories. Snippets in CodeKeep can be organized using labels and folders, and the interface lets users filter their snippet library by label to quickly locate a piece of code they saved previously, which is the core workflow the product is built around: capture a snippet once, tag it, and find it again in seconds rather than re-writing or re-searching for it later. Beyond storage and organization, CodeKeep includes browser and editor tooling meant to reduce friction around getting code in and out of the tool: a Visual Studio Code extension and a Google Chrome extension let users import and use snippets without leaving their existing workflow, so a snippet captured on the web can be pulled directly into an editor, and code written in an editor can be pushed into the CodeKeep library without manual copy-paste. A distinguishing feature is CodeKeep's built-in screenshot editor, which turns a saved snippet into a shareable, styled image, letting users choose from templates, backgrounds, and image treatments, aimed at developers who want to share code snippets on social platforms, in documentation, or in presentations with better visual presentation than a plain-text paste. The interface also supports dark mode, and the platform includes a discovery or browsing feature where users can find snippets other members of the community have chosen to share publicly, turning CodeKeep into a lightweight repository of reusable, community-sourced code fragments in addition to a private organization tool. Commercially, CodeKeep follows a freemium subscription model: a free tier exists for basic personal use, and paid plans start at 4.99 US dollars per month, unlocking expanded storage, organization, or sharing capacity, though CodeKeep does not publish extensive public detail on exact tier-by-tier feature breakdowns beyond the entry price point. The company behind CodeKeep does not publish its founding year, headquarters, or team size on its main marketing pages, so those details should be treated as not publicly disclosed rather than assumed; third-party software directories list it as aimed at individuals and medium-sized businesses rather than large enterprises. Typical users are individual developers who repeatedly reuse boilerplate code, configuration snippets, regex patterns, or common utility functions across projects, technical writers and educators who need a fast way to turn code into a shareable image for tutorials or social posts, and small teams that want a shared, searchable snippet library without adopting a heavier internal-wiki or documentation platform. Compared to alternatives like GitHub Gist, IDE-native snippet managers, or dedicated tools such as SnippetsLab and Boostnote, CodeKeep's main differentiators are its label and folder organization model, its VSCode and Chrome extensions for low-friction capture, and its screenshot-styling feature for visually sharing code.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Snippet Organization and Capture Work
paragraphs: CodeKeep organizes snippets using folders and labels, letting users filter their library by label to quickly relocate a piece of code they saved earlier, the core capture-and-reuse workflow the product is built around.

A Visual Studio Code extension and a Google Chrome extension reduce copy-paste friction, letting users pull snippets directly into an editor or push editor code into the CodeKeep library without leaving their existing workflow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Turning Snippets Into Shareable Images
paragraphs: A built-in screenshot editor turns any saved snippet into a styled, shareable image, with templates, backgrounds, and image treatments for use in documentation, presentations, or social posts.
The platform also includes a public discovery feature where users can browse snippets other members have chosen to share, adding a community dimension on top of private organization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and What Is Not Publicly Disclosed
paragraphs: CodeKeep uses a freemium model: a free tier covers basic personal use, and paid plans start at 4.99 US dollars per month for expanded capacity.
The company does not publish its founding year, headquarters, or team size on its main marketing pages; those details should be treated as not publicly disclosed.

Screenshot styling feature is unusual among snippet managers
Simple, notes-app-like capture workflow lowers the learning curve
A free tier is available for basic personal use before committing to a paid plan
cons: Company founding year, headquarters, and team size are not publicly disclosed
Smaller ecosystem and integration list than GitHub Gist or IDE-native snippet tools
Detailed feature-by-tier pricing breakdown is not fully published
Public snippet discovery may not suit teams needing strictly private storage
Less suited to large enterprise teams than to individuals and small businesses

faqs.question: What is CodeKeep?
faqs.answer: A code snippet manager for saving, organizing, and sharing reusable code and text.

faqs.question: How much does CodeKeep cost?
faqs.answer: A free tier is available; paid plans start at 4.99 US dollars per month.

faqs.question: Does CodeKeep have a VSCode extension?
faqs.answer: Yes, plus a Google Chrome extension for web-based capture.

faqs.question: Can I organize snippets into folders in CodeKeep?
faqs.answer: Yes, via folders and labels, with filtering by label.

faqs.question: Does CodeKeep let me share snippets as images?
faqs.answer: Yes, via its built-in screenshot editor with templates and backgrounds.

faqs.question: Is CodeKeep only for individuals?
faqs.answer: It targets both individuals and small to medium-sized teams.

faqs.question: Where is CodeKeep based?
faqs.answer: Not publicly disclosed.

faqs.question: Can other users discover snippets I share publicly?
faqs.answer: Yes, CodeKeep includes a public snippet discovery feature.

enrichment.short_description: Codemagic is a CI/CD platform purpose-built for mobile app development, supporting Flutter, React Native, iOS (Swift and Objective-C), and Android (Kotlin and Java) projects. It is developed by Nevercode, an Estonian company whose roots trace to a 2012 hackathon in Tartu, and Codemagic has an official partnership with Google as a recommended CI/CD tool for the Flutter ecosystem. It offers usage-based, fixed, and enterprise pricing built around macOS build minutes and concurrency.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium with usage-based pay-as-you-go, fixed-price annual, and custom enterprise tiers
enrichment.starting_price: Free (500 macOS M2 build minutes per month); pay-as-you-go from about $0.045 to $0.114 per build minute depending on machine type
enrichment.company_size: Team members across 15-plus countries; exact headcount not publicly disclosed
enrichment.seo_title: Codemagic Review 2026: Pricing and Mobile CI/CD Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What Codemagic does for Flutter, React Native, iOS and Android CI/CD, its 2026 pricing plans, key features, pros, cons and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Codemagic is a continuous integration and continuous delivery platform built specifically for mobile application development, supporting Flutter, React Native, native iOS (Swift and Objective-C), and native Android (Kotlin and Java) projects. It is developed and operated by Nevercode, a company whose history traces back to 2012, when an internal hackathon at Mobi Lab, a development and design studio in Tartu, Estonia, produced a mobile-focused build tool originally called Greenhouse. Greenhouse shipped as a product in 2014 and the company rebranded to Nevercode in 2017 as a general mobile CI/CD service. In December 2018, at the Flutter Live event, Nevercode launched Codemagic as the first CI/CD tool built exclusively for Flutter applications, offering it free to everyone during its first several months before introducing paid pricing for professional developers and teams in May 2019. Codemagic has an official partnership with Google, under which it is promoted as a recommended CI/CD solution for Flutter developers, though Nevercode continues to operate Codemagic as its own company rather than as a Google-owned product; the company describes having team members across 15 or more countries. Codemagic's core workflow lets teams connect a mobile app repository, define a build pipeline through either a visual workflow editor or a codemagic.yaml configuration file, and run automated builds, tests, code signing, and store submissions across macOS, Linux, and Windows build machines, with results and artifacts, including IPA and APK files, delivered automatically to testers or app store review queues. Because iOS builds require macOS hardware, Codemagic's build-minute pricing is largely organized around which Mac machine tier a build runs on. As of 2026, Codemagic offers four main pricing structures for its core mobile CI/CD product. The Free plan provides 500 macOS M2 build minutes per month, refilled monthly, with one parallel build, one team member, 30-day build history, and community support. The Pay as You Go plan has no monthly base fee and instead charges per build minute: 0.095 US dollars per minute on a Mac mini M2, 0.114 US dollars per minute on a Mac mini M4, and 0.045 US dollars per minute on Linux or Windows machines, with additional concurrency available for 49 US dollars per month, app-preview builds billed at 0.095 US dollars per minute, unlimited team members, and 60-day artifact storage. Fixed Price plans trade usage-based billing for a flat annual fee with unlimited build minutes and three concurrent builds: 3,990 US dollars per year on the M2 plan, 5,400 US dollars per year on the M4 plan, and 9,000 US dollars per year on the M4 Max plan, with additional concurrency available for 1,500 to 3,000 US dollars per year depending on machine tier. The Enterprise plan starts at 12,000 US dollars per year and adds custom features, dedicated support, single sign-on via OpenID, OAuth2, or SAML 2.0, and signed SLAs, NDAs, and DPAs for regulated organizations. Codemagic separately prices its React Native CodePush over-the-air update service, either pay-as-you-go at 1 US dollar per 2,500 installs with no transfer or storage fees, or a fixed plan starting at 99 US dollars per month, billed per 100,000 monthly active users, with unlimited releases and included transfer and storage. Typical users are Flutter and React Native teams that need automated builds, testing, and code signing across both Apple and Google app stores, as well as native iOS and Android teams that want a mobile-specialized alternative to general-purpose CI providers like GitHub Actions, Bitrise, or CircleCI.

long_form_blocks.heading: From a 2012 Hackathon to Google's Flutter Partner
paragraphs: Codemagic traces to a 2012 internal hackathon at Mobi Lab in Tartu, Estonia, which produced Greenhouse, released as a product in 2014 and rebranded to Nevercode in 2017.

In December 2018, Nevercode launched Codemagic as the first CI/CD tool built exclusively for Flutter, and later formed an official partnership with Google that positions Codemagic as a recommended Flutter CI/CD tool, while Nevercode continues to operate it independently.

long_form_blocks.heading: Build Pipelines, Code Signing, and Store Delivery
paragraphs: Teams connect a mobile repository and define pipelines via a visual editor or a codemagic.yaml file, running builds, tests, code signing, and store submissions across macOS, Linux, and Windows machines.

Because iOS builds require macOS hardware, pricing is largely organized around which Mac machine tier, M2 or M4, a build runs on, with results delivered automatically to testers or app store review queues.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pay-as-You-Go, Fixed, and Enterprise Pricing
paragraphs: Pay-as-you-go bills per build minute, from 0.045 dollars on Linux or Windows up to 0.114 dollars on Mac mini M4, while Fixed Price plans offer unlimited build minutes for a flat annual fee starting at 3,990 dollars.

Enterprise pricing starts at 12,000 dollars per year and adds SSO, dedicated support, and signed SLAs, NDAs, and DPAs, while a separate CodePush pricing track covers React Native over-the-air updates.

pros: Purpose-built for mobile, reducing setup complexity versus general CI tools
Official Google partnership with strong Flutter-specific tooling
Flexible pricing spans free, pay-as-you-go, fixed annual, and enterprise tiers
Automated code signing and store submission save significant setup time
Supports native iOS and Android as well as Flutter and React Native in one platform
cons: iOS builds require macOS minutes, which are priced higher than Linux or Windows
Fixed-price and Enterprise plans require a significant annual budget commitment
Free plan's 500 minutes and single concurrency are limiting for active teams
Pay-as-you-go costs can become unpredictable for high-frequency build teams
CodePush pricing is a separate line item from core CI/CD pricing

faqs.answer: Flutter, React Native, native iOS, and native Android.

faqs.question: Who owns Codemagic?
faqs.answer: It is developed by Nevercode, based in Tartu, Estonia, and has an official partnership with Google.

faqs.question: Is there a free Codemagic plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, 500 macOS M2 build minutes per month with one parallel build and one team member.

faqs.question: How is Codemagic priced?
faqs.answer: Free, pay-as-you-go per build minute, fixed annual plans, or custom Enterprise pricing.

faqs.question: Does Codemagic support automated iOS code signing?
faqs.answer: Yes, code signing and app store submission are automated.

faqs.question: What is Codemagic CodePush?
faqs.answer: A separately priced over-the-air update service for React Native apps.

faqs.question: Can I use Codemagic without owning a Mac?
faqs.answer: Yes, it provides macOS build machines in the cloud for iOS builds.

faqs.question: Does Codemagic offer SSO for enterprise teams?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenID, OAuth2, and SAML 2.0 are available on the Enterprise plan.

enrichment.short_description: Codename One is an open-source, write-once-run-anywhere framework that lets Java and Kotlin developers build truly native mobile apps for iOS, Android, desktop, and web from a single codebase. It was founded in 2012 by Chen Fishbein and Shai Almog, former Sun Microsystems engineers who had previously created the LWUIT toolkit, and it compiles Java bytecode to native platform executables using cloud build servers. The core framework is free and open source, with optional paid cloud-build plans for higher quotas and advanced features.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open-source core framework (free) plus optional paid cloud build service tiers priced per developer seat
enrichment.starting_price: Free (100 build credits per month); paid plans start at $25 per month (Basic)
enrichment.company_size: Small, founder-led team; exact headcount not publicly disclosed
enrichment.seo_title: Codename One Review 2026: Pricing and Framework Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What Codename One is, its Java and Kotlin cross-platform mobile framework, 2026 cloud build pricing, key features, pros, cons and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Codename One is an open-source, write-once-run-anywhere framework that lets developers build truly native applications for iOS, Android, desktop, web, and other platforms from a single Java or Kotlin codebase. It was founded in January 2012 by Chen Fishbein and Shai Almog, two engineers who had previously worked at Sun Microsystems, where in 2007 they created the Lightweight User Interface Toolkit, an open-source UI library aimed at simplifying Java ME mobile development. After leaving Oracle, which had acquired Sun, Fishbein and Almog founded Codename One as an independent, free alternative to proprietary cross-platform mobile tools, building on the component and abstraction ideas from LWUIT and adding a device simulator plus cloud-based build servers capable of compiling Java bytecode into native platform executables. The beta release in mid-2012 quickly passed 100,000 SDK downloads, reflecting demand at the time for tools that let Java developers reach iOS and Android without learning Objective-C or platform-specific Android tooling. Technically, Codename One combines a portable application runtime and UI toolkit with native compilers, per-platform ports, build tooling, and direct access to native SDK capabilities, allowing a single Java or Kotlin codebase to target iOS, Android, desktop operating systems, the web via a browser-based port, and other device categories, including TVs and wearables, without maintaining separate native codebases per platform. Because compiling native iOS binaries typically requires Apple's Xcode toolchain and macOS hardware, Codename One's cloud build service is central to its value proposition: it lets developers on Windows or Linux machines build native iOS apps without owning a Mac, by sending the project to Codename One's build servers. The core framework itself is fully open source and can be used commercially or non-commercially with no royalties, licensing fees, or per-app charges, which distinguishes it from some proprietary cross-platform tools that take a revenue cut or require enterprise contracts for commercial use. Codename One's commercial layer sits entirely around the optional cloud build service, which is priced per developer seat rather than per application. As of 2026, the Free plan includes 100 build credits per month, with an iOS cloud build consuming 8 credits and a maximum user JAR size of 8.5 megabytes, workable for evaluation and small projects. The Basic plan costs 25 US dollars per month, or 300 US dollars if billed annually, and unlocks unlimited build credits, access to native platform sources, a 50 megabyte build JAR size limit, 5,000 push notifications per month, 5 automated store submissions per month, two weeks of build versioning history, and in-app commerce validation up to 10,000 US dollars per month. The Pro plan costs 85 US dollars per month, or 1,020 US dollars annually, and adds concurrent cloud builds, GitHub Issues-integrated crash protection, 1 million push notifications per month, email support, native desktop app builds, 50 automated submissions per month, and commerce validation up to 200,000 US dollars per month. The Enterprise plan costs 450 US dollars per month, or 5,400 US dollars annually, and adds high-priority builds and CI, on-device unit testing, 10 million push notifications per month, phone support, unlimited automated submissions, and unlimited commerce validation. All paid plans carry a 30-day money-back guarantee. Codename One is typically chosen by teams and individual developers with existing Java or Kotlin expertise, including enterprise Java shops, who want to reach mobile and desktop platforms without adopting Swift, Kotlin Multiplatform-only tooling, Flutter and Dart, or React Native's JavaScript-centric stack, and by developers who specifically need to produce native iOS builds without owning Apple hardware.

long_form_blocks.heading: From Sun Microsystems LWUIT to Codename One
paragraphs: Codename One was founded in January 2012 by Chen Fishbein and Shai Almog, former Sun Microsystems engineers who in 2007 created the Lightweight User Interface Toolkit to simplify Java ME mobile development.
After leaving Oracle, the pair built Codename One as an independent, free alternative to proprietary cross-platform tools, and its 2012 beta release quickly passed 100,000 SDK downloads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Write Once, Run Anywhere: The Technical Model
paragraphs: Codename One combines a portable application runtime and UI toolkit with native compilers, per-platform ports, and direct access to native SDK capabilities, letting one Java or Kotlin codebase target iOS, Android, desktop, web, and more.
Its cloud build servers compile Java bytecode into native iOS binaries, letting developers on Windows or Linux build native iOS apps without owning a Mac.

long_form_blocks.heading: Free Framework, Paid Cloud Build Tiers
paragraphs: The core framework is free, open source, and carries no royalties or per-app fees for commercial or non-commercial use.
The optional cloud build service is priced per developer seat across Free, Basic, Pro, and Enterprise tiers, scaling build credits, push notification volume, submission automation, and support level.

features.title: Single Java/Kotlin codebase for multiple platforms
features.description: Targets iOS, Android, desktop, and web from one codebase.

features.title: Cloud build servers
features.description: Produce native iOS binaries without requiring a Mac, from Windows or Linux.

features.title: Device simulator
features.description: Test applications locally before running a native cloud build.

features.title: Visual theme and UI builder
features.description: Design tools for building and customizing app interfaces.

features.title: Native SDK access
features.description: Java and Kotlin code can call native platform SDK capabilities directly.

features.title: IDE integrations
features.description: Works with common Java development environments.

features.title: In-app commerce validation
features.description: Validates in-app purchases up to plan-specific monthly limits.

features.title: Scaled push notifications
features.description: Delivers push notifications at volumes that scale with the paid plan.

pros: Core framework is fully open source with no royalties or per-app fees
Cloud build service enables native iOS builds from Windows or Linux
Long track record dating to 2012, with roots in Sun Microsystems' LWUIT
Lets existing Java and Kotlin teams reach mobile without learning new languages
Tiered pricing scales from a free evaluation tier to enterprise support
cons: Java and Kotlin UI paradigm is less mainstream for mobile today than Swift, Kotlin-native, or Flutter and Dart
Smaller community and hiring pool than Flutter or React Native
Company headquarters and team size are not publicly disclosed
Free tier's build-credit limits make it unsuitable for active development
Native look-and-feel parity requires more manual theming than some modern frameworks

faqs.question: What languages does Codename One use?
faqs.answer: Java and Kotlin.

faqs.question: Is Codename One free?
faqs.answer: The core framework is open source and free; the optional cloud build service has paid tiers.

faqs.question: Can I build iOS apps on Windows or Linux with Codename One?
faqs.answer: Yes, via its cloud build service, without owning a Mac.

faqs.question: Who created Codename One?
faqs.answer: Chen Fishbein and Shai Almog, in 2012.

faqs.question: What platforms does Codename One target?
faqs.answer: iOS, Android, desktop, web, and other device categories.

faqs.question: Does Codename One charge royalties on commercial apps?
faqs.answer: No, there are no royalties or per-app fees.

faqs.question: What is included in the Free cloud build plan?
faqs.answer: 100 build credits per month, with an 8.5MB user JAR size limit.

faqs.question: Is there a money-back guarantee on paid plans?
faqs.answer: Yes, a 30-day money-back guarantee applies to paid plans.

use_cases.title: Enterprise Java mobile expansion
use_cases.description: An enterprise Java team extends an existing codebase to mobile platforms.

use_cases.title: iOS builds without a Mac
use_cases.description: A Windows or Linux-based developer produces native iOS builds via cloud build servers.

use_cases.title: Single codebase across platforms
use_cases.description: A small team builds one codebase targeting iOS, Android, and desktop simultaneously.
use_cases.audience: Small mobile development teams and indie studios
